class="col-span-12 lg:col-span-10 lg:col-start-2 mt-4"><div class="grid gap-8 lg:grid-cols-12"><div class="min-w-0 lg:col-span-9"><div class="copy-lg mb-4 whitespace-pre-line font-sans text-foreground">An agent operating system is a coordination layer that manages memory, tools, scheduling, and access control for AI agents so they can work reliably across complex, multi-step tasks. This guide covers how they work, what they're built from, and what changes when entire organizations start running agents at scale.</div><h3 id="_tl_dr" class="mb-4 mt-8 scroll-mt-20 text-foreground heading-xl lg:heading-2xl xl:heading-3xl py-1 text-left font-sans"><strong class="font-semibold">📌 TL;DR</strong></h3><div class="copy-lg mb-4 whitespace-pre-line font-sans text-foreground"><strong class="font-semibold">Short on time? Here are the key takeaways:</strong></div><ul class="mb-4 list-disc space-y-2 pl-6"><li class="pl-1"><div class="copy-lg mb-4 whitespace-pre-line font-sans text-foreground"><strong class="font-semibold">How agent OS works:</strong> Agents receive tasks, retrieve context from memory, plan and execute using available tools, coordinate with other agents when needed, and log every action for auditability.</div></li><li class="pl-1"><div class="copy-lg mb-4 whitespace-pre-line font-sans text-foreground"><strong class="font-semibold">Four core components:</strong> Memory management, scheduling and orchestration, tool management, and security and access control form the foundation of any functional agent OS.</div></li><li class="pl-1"><div class="copy-lg mb-4 whitespace-pre-line font-sans text-foreground"><strong class="font-semibold">The key difference from automation:</strong> Modern automation tools add AI steps to a flow you define. An agent OS lets the agent define the flow.</div></li><li class="pl-1"><div class="copy-lg mb-4 whitespace-pre-line font-sans text-foreground"><strong class="font-semibold">Multiplayer AI:</strong> At company scale, agents need shared knowledge, unified governance, and improvements that compound across teams. <strong class="font-semibold">Dust</strong> takes this approach, giving people and agents a shared workspace across the whole organization.</div></li></ul><hr><h3 id="how_agent_operating_systems_work" class="mb-4 mt-8 scroll-mt-20 text-foreground heading-xl lg:heading-2xl xl:heading-3xl py-1 text-left font-sans"><strong class="font-semibold">How agent operating systems work</strong></h3><div class="copy-lg mb-4 whitespace-pre-line font-sans text-foreground"><strong class="font-semibold">An agent operating system is a coordination layer that sits above a traditional operating system, giving AI agents structured access to memory, tools, and system resources while managing how they execute tasks.</strong> Where a conventional OS allocates CPU cycles and memory between software processes, an agent OS does the same for AI agents, with the additional challenge of managing context, reasoning state, and execution loops rather than deterministic program instructions.</div><div class="copy-lg mb-4 whitespace-pre-line font-sans text-foreground">The execution flow looks like this:</div><ul class="mb-4 list-disc space-y-2 pl-6"><li class="pl-1"><div class="copy-lg mb-4 whitespace-pre-line font-sans text-foreground"><strong class="font-semibold">Task intake:</strong> A request arrives from a user, a scheduled trigger, or an upstream system. The agent OS determines which agent or agents should handle it based on role definitions and available resources.</div></li><li class="pl-1"><div class="copy-lg mb-4 whitespace-pre-line font-sans text-foreground"><strong class="font-semibold">Memory retrieval:</strong> Relevant context is fetched from short-term memory (the current session) and long-term memory (persisted knowledge, past OS</strong></h3><div class="copy-lg mb-4 whitespace-pre-line font-sans text-foreground">The core components of an agent operating system are the building blocks that make AI agents reliable, persistent, and governable across complex tasks.</div><div class="copy-lg mb-4 whitespace-pre-line font-sans text-foreground">Each component handles a different dimension of context, from what an agent knows and remembers to what it can do and who controls access.</div><h4 id="memory_management" class="mb-3 mt-6 scroll-mt-20 text-foreground heading-lg lg:heading-xl xl:heading-2xl text-left font-sans"><strong class="font-semibold">Memory management</strong></h4><div class="copy-lg mb-4 whitespace-pre-line font-sans text-foreground">Memory management is what gives AI agents continuity across a session and across time. Without it, every agent call starts from zero, with no knowledge of prior decisions, user preferences, or task history.</div><div class="copy-lg mb-4 whitespace-pre-line font-sans text-foreground">In practice, agent operating systems maintain two kinds of memory: short-term memory, which holds the active context for the current task and is bounded by the model's context window, and long-term memory, which persists across sessions in databases or vector stores.</div><div class="copy-lg mb-4 whitespace-pre-line font-sans text-foreground">Getting memory right means storing conclusions and decisions rather than full transcripts, which keeps retrieval relevant and reduces noise over time.</div><h4 id="scheduling_and_orchestration" class="mb-3 mt-6 scroll-mt-20 text-foreground heading-lg lg:heading-xl xl:heading-2xl text-left font-sans"><strong class="font-semibold">Scheduling and orchestration</strong></h4><div class="copy-lg mb-4 whitespace-pre-line font-sans text-foreground">Scheduling and orchestration determine which agents run, when they run, and in what order. In a single-agent system, orchestration is simpler, typically a single reasoning loop with tool access, but multi-agent setups require a full coordination layer that routes tasks to the appropriate agent, manages dependencies between steps, and handles parallel execution where tasks can run simultaneously without waiting for each other.</div><div class="copy-lg mb-4 whitespace-pre-line font-sans text-foreground">The orchestration layer also defines what happens when something goes wrong, whether a sub-agent fails or returns an unexpected output. Centralized orchestrators are easier to debug; decentralized models are more resilient to single points of failure. Most production deployments use a combination of both.</div><h4 id="tool_management" class="mb-3 mt-6 scroll-mt-20 text-foreground heading-lg lg:heading-xl xl:heading-2xl text-left font-sans"><strong class="font-semibold">Tool management</strong></h4><div class="copy-lg mb-4 whitespace-pre-line font-sans text-foreground">Tool management defines the set of capabilities an agent can call on to take action beyond generating text. These capabilities cover data retrieval (searches, database queries, API reads), data mutation (writing records, updating fields, sending messages), computation, and communication with external systems.</div><div class="copy-lg mb-4 whitespace-pre-line font-sans text-foreground">How tools are defined matters as much as which tools are available: an agent that doesn't understand what a tool does, or what inputs it expects, will use it incorrectly or skip it when it would be useful. The best tool definitions are purposeful and well-described, with clear names, typed inputs, and predictable outputs. Best practices favor fewer, higher-level tools that consolidate related operations over many narrow primitives. This reduces selection errors and keeps the agent's reasoning chain focused at each step.</div><aside class="my-10 flex justify-center"><div class="flex flex-wrap items-center justify-center gap-3"><a target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" href="https://dust.tt/home/contact"><span class="s-inline-flex s-items-center s-justify-center s-whitespace-nowrap s-ring-offset-background s-transition-colors s-ring-inset s-select-none focus-visible:s-outline-none focus-visible:s-ring-2 focus-visible:s-ring-ring focus-visible:s-ring-offset-0 dark:focus-visible:s-ring-0 dark:focus-visible:s-ring-offset-1 s-border s-border-transparent s-bg-highlight s-text-highlight-50 hover:s-bg-highlight-light active:s-bg-highlight-dark disabled:s-bg-highlight-muted disabled:s-text-highlight-50/60 dark:disabled:s-bg-highlight-muted-night s-h-9 s-px-3 s-gap-2 s-shrink-0 s-rounded-xl" aria-label="Request a Demo"><div class="s-flex s-items-center s-gap-2 s-label-sm">Request a Demo</div></span></a>

class="font-semibold">Frequently asked questions (FAQs)</strong></h3><h4 id="what_is_an_agent_operating_system_and_how_is_it_different_from_a_regular_os_" class="mb-3 mt-6 scroll-mt-20 text-foreground heading-lg lg:heading-xl xl:heading-2xl text-left font-sans"><strong class="font-semibold">What is an agent operating system, and how is it different from a regular OS?</strong></h4><div class="copy-lg mb-4 whitespace-pre-line font-sans text-foreground">An agent operating system is a software layer designed to coordinate AI agents rather than hardware processes. A conventional OS allocates CPU, memory, and I/O resources between software programs. An agent OS does something similar but for LLM-based agents, managing context windows, tool access, memory persistence, and inter-agent communication. The core difference is that traditional OS operations are deterministic: a process either gets resources or it doesn't. Agent OS operations involve reasoning loops, non-deterministic outputs, and stateful context that must be maintained across multiple steps and, in many cases, multiple sessions.</div><h4 id="do_you_need_an_agent_operating_system_for_a_simple_ai_workflow_" class="mb-3 mt-6 scroll-mt-20 text-foreground heading-lg lg:heading-xl xl:heading-2xl text-left font-sans"><strong class="font-semibold">Do you need an agent operating system for a simple AI workflow?</strong></h4><div class="copy-lg mb-4 whitespace-pre-line font-sans text-foreground">Not always. A single-agent workflow with a narrow scope and a few tools may only need a clear role definition, basic tool access, and some guardrails to operate reliably. The agent OS model becomes relevant when complexity grows: when multiple agents need to coordinate, when context must persist across sessions, when different users or teams interact with the same agents, or when the system handles sensitive data that requires governed access.</div><h4 id="how_does_memory_work_in_an_agent_operating_system_" class="mb-3 mt-6 scroll-mt-20 text-foreground heading-lg lg:heading-xl xl:heading-2xl text-left font-sans"><strong class="font-semibold">How does memory work in an agent operating system?</strong></h4><div class="copy-lg mb-4 whitespace-pre-line font-sans text-foreground">Memory in an agent OS works at two levels. Short-term memory is the active context window: everything the agent can see during the current task, including the conversation, recent tool outputs, and intermediate reasoning steps. Long-term memory persists across sessions, stored in databases or vector stores and retrieved when the agent needs historical context. In multi-agent systems, memory management also includes structured handoffs: summaries of what one agent accomplished and what the next agent needs to know to continue without repeating work. Storing conclusions and decisions rather than full transcripts keeps retrieval precise and reduces noise.</div></div><div class="hidden lg:col-span-3 lg:block"><div class="sticky top-5 flex The infrastructure challenge becomes not just how one agent manages memory and tools, but how an organization governs agents across all its teams at once.","nodeType":"text"}],"nodeType":"paragraph"},{"data":{},"content":[{"data":{},"marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"value":"Dust","nodeType":"text"},{"data":{},"marks":[],"value":" takes a different approach: a multiplayer AI workspace where teams and agents share the same knowledge, tools, and context across the organization. A conventional OS allocates CPU, memory, and I/O resources between software programs. An agent OS does something similar but for LLM-based agents, managing context windows, tool access, memory persistence, and inter-agent communication. The core difference is that traditional OS operations are deterministic: a process either gets resources or it doesn't. Agent OS operations involve reasoning loops, non-deterministic outputs, and stateful context that must be maintained across multiple steps and, in many cases, multiple sessions.","nodeType":"text"}],"nodeType":"paragraph"},{"data":{},"content":[{"data":{},"marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"value":"Do you need an agent operating system for a simple AI workflow?","nodeType":"text"}],"nodeType":"heading-3"},{"data":{},"content":[{"data":{},"marks":[],"value":"Not always. A single-agent workflow with a narrow scope and a few tools may only need a clear role definition, basic tool access, and some guardrails to operate reliably. The agent OS model becomes relevant when complexity grows: when multiple agents need to coordinate, when context must persist across sessions, when different users or teams interact with the same agents, or when the system handles sensitive data that requires governed access.","nodeType":"text"}],"nodeType":"paragraph"},{"data":{},"content":[{"data":{},"marks":[{"type":"bold"}],"value":"How does memory work in an agent operating system?","nodeType":"text"}],"nodeType":"heading-3"},{"data":{},"content":[{"data":{},"marks":[],"value":"Memory in an agent OS works at two levels. Short-term memory is the active context window: everything the agent can see during the current task, including the conversation, recent tool outputs, and intermediate reasoning steps. Long-term memory persists across sessions, stored in databases or vector stores and retrieved when the agent needs historical context. In multi-agent systems, memory management also includes structured handoffs: summaries of what one agent accomplished and what the next agent needs to know to continue without repeating work. Storing conclusions and decisions rather than full transcripts keeps retrieval precise and reduces
